source.subject: Opsly pilot: preferred deployment path and sandbox endpoints next steps
messages:
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Alicia Tran <alicia.tran@opsly.ai>, Devon Lee <devon@opsly.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 1 Aug 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Opsly pilot: deployment preference & quick sandbox endpoints

Hi Alicia / Devon —

Thanks again for taking the demo this morning. Quick recap and a short set of asks so we can scope a Hosted pilot vs Dedicated/Dedicated-onboarding path: 

What we heard:
- Primary goal: integrate Redwood for real-time support reply generation inside Opsly’s admin console.
- Desired starting mode: you indicated wanting lowest ops friction — likely Hosted API for an initial pilot.
- Long-term: consider Dedicated GPU pool if traffic grows or for stricter tenancy.

To move fast, can you confirm the following by end of week?
1) Pilot traffic profile (qps and average tokens per request) — sample day or expected peak.
2) Any hard SLOs for P95 latency (e.g., <250ms) or strict data residency needs.
3) Stakeholders for procurement/security (who would receive SOC2 artifacts / SLA ask).

Next we’ll:
- Share a sandbox endpoint map and a short quickstart workbook (attached) that shows endpoints, example client config, and conservative rate limits for the Hosted trial.
- Karthik will outline how we handle prefix/KV caching and burst capacity so you get predictable latency while keeping cost down.

Attachments: redwood-quickstart-endpoint-map.xlsx (sandbox endpoints + sample cURLs)
Also attaching Opsly_SLA_Request.pdf so you can annotate any specific legal/SLA asks.

Proposed next step: 30-min ops call with Karthik + your dev lead to validate the traffic profile and confirm Hosted quickstart scope. Avail Tue/Thu next week?

Thanks — looking forward to getting Opsly into a short Hosted pilot.

—
Marissa Cole
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0183
Redwood Inference

From: Alicia Tran <alicia.tran@opsly.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Devon Lee <devon@opsly.ai>
Date: Wed, 1 Aug 2028 11:40:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Opsly pilot: deployment preference & quick sandbox endpoints

Marissa — thanks for the clear recap. Quick answers inline.

> Pilot traffic profile (qps and average tokens per request)
We expect ~10 req/s average, with short bursts to 40 req/s during peak (US morning). Typical prompt + completion is ~600 tokens total.

> P95 latency SLO
We'd like P95 < 350ms for the initial pilot; long-term we'd target <250ms.

> Stakeholders for procurement/security
Procurement: Devon Lee (cc). Security: I handle initial reviews, but we’ll need SOC2 artifacts for our legal/compliance team.

Hosted sounds right to start. A couple of quick questions before we lock the ops call: 
- Are sandbox endpoints client-isolated (separate keys) so we can safely send redacted production-like traffic?
- What are conservative rate limits you recommend out of the box for the Hosted sandbox?

Appreciate the attachments — I’ll share Opsly_SLA_Request.pdf with legal and annotate. Tue 8/8 at 10am PT works for our dev lead; is that available on your side?

Thanks,
Alicia
Head of Product, Opsly
+1 (628) 555-0220

On Wed, Aug 1, 2028 at 09:12 AM Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com> wrote:

> 
> Thanks again for taking the demo this morning. Quick recap and a short set of asks so we can scope a Hosted pilot vs Dedicated/Dedicated-onboarding path: 
> 
> What we heard:

> - Desired starting mode: you indicated wanting lowest ops friction — likely Hosted API for an initial pilot.
> - Long-term: consider Dedicated GPU pool if traffic grows or for stricter tenancy.
> 
> To move fast, can you confirm the following by end of week?
> 1) Pilot traffic profile (qps and average tokens per request) — sample day or expected peak.
> 2) Any hard SLOs for P95 latency (e.g., <250ms) or strict data residency needs.

> Also attaching Opsly_SLA_Request.pdf so you can annotate any specific legal/SLA asks.
> 
> Proposed next step: 30-min ops call with Karthik + your dev lead to validate the traffic profile and confirm Hosted quickstart scope. Avail Tue/Thu next week?
> 
> Thanks — looking forward to getting Opsly into a short Hosted pilot.
> 
> —
> Marissa Cole
> Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
> marissa.cole@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0183
> Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Alicia Tran <alicia.tran@opsly.ai>, Devon Lee <devon@opsly.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 1 Aug 2028 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Opsly pilot: deployment preference & quick sandbox endpoints

Hi Alicia — quick technical reply to the two questions you called out.

1) Sandbox isolation / keys
- Yes: Hosted sandbox workspaces are client-isolated. We provision a separate API key and sandbox project for Opsly so you can run redacted production-like traffic without cross-tenant exposure. Keys can be rotated and scoped to sandbox endpoints.

2) Conservative Hosted rate limits (recommended initial config)
- Baseline: 20 requests/sec aggregated for the sandbox project (shared across endpoints).
- Burst window: allow up to 60 req/sec sustained for short bursts (30s) with queued backpressure beyond that.
- Per-route token cap: 2,000 tokens per request to prevent runaway costs during pilot.

Additional details:
- Latency: with prefix/KV caching enabled we typically see median latency drop 30–40% for repetitive prompts; P95 at the traffic profile you shared (10 avg / 40 peak, ~600 tokens) should be in the ~200–320ms window on Hosted depending on model choice.
- If you need stricter tenancy or reserved throughput (no noisy neighbors), we can outline a Dedicated commitment path — that typically includes reserved GPU pools and a migration playbook (we can share that if you want to see costs/lead times).

Attached is a slimmed endpoint map (same workbook Marissa attached) and a short checklist for the ops call. Can we lock Tue 8/8 10am PT for the 30-min call? I’ll prepare sample cURLs and a small test script to simulate your peak bursts.

—
Karthik Iyer
Principal Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0137

On Wed, Aug 1, 2028 at 11:40 AM Alicia Tran <alicia.tran@opsly.ai> wrote:
> Marissa — thanks for the clear recap. Quick answers inline.
> 
> > Pilot traffic profile (qps and average tokens per request)
> We expect ~10 req/s average, with short bursts to 40 req/s during peak (US morning). Typical prompt + completion is ~600 tokens total.
> 
> > P95 latency SLO
> We'd like P95 < 350ms for the initial pilot; long-term we'd target <250ms.
> 
> > Stakeholders for procurement/security
> Procurement: Devon Lee (cc). Security: I handle initial reviews, but we’ll need SOC2 artifacts for our legal/compliance team.
> 
> Hosted sounds right to start. A couple of quick questions before we lock the ops call: 
> - Are sandbox endpoints client-isolated (separate keys) so we can safely send redacted production-like traffic?
> - What are conservative rate limits you recommend out of the box for the Hosted sandbox?
> 
> Appreciate the attachments — I’ll share Opsly_SLA_Request.pdf with legal and annotate. Tue 8/8 at 10am PT works for our dev lead; is that available on your side?
> 
> Thanks,
> Alicia
> Head of Product, Opsly

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Alicia Tran <alicia.tran@opsly.ai>, Devon Lee <devon@opsly.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 2 Aug 2028 16:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Opsly pilot: deployment preference & quick sandbox endpoints — confirmed next steps

Great — thanks everyone. Confirming the mutual action plan and a few concrete next steps: 

Mutual action plan (short pilot):
- By 8/3: Opsly to send a sample day traffic export (CSV) and confirm dev lead on the call (Alicia noted dev lead + Devon).
- 8/8 10:00am PT: 30-min ops call (Karthik + Opsly dev lead) — Karthik will walk the quickstart, sandbox keys, and run the burst script.
- 8/9–8/16: Short hosted pilot window (2x weeks) — Redwood will monitor P95 and advise cache tuning.
- 8/20: checkpoint — review metrics and decide whether to continue on Hosted or evaluate Dedicated sizing.

Action items for Opsly:
- Share the sample traffic CSV and any legal annotations to Opsly_SLA_Request.pdf.
- Confirm participants for the 8/8 call.

Action items for Redwood:
- Karthik: provide sandbox key creation steps, conservative rate-limit defaults, and the burst test script.
- Marissa/SE team: create a short HubSpot task with the trial timeline and attach the annotated SLA after you share it. Link below.

HubSpot: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/12345/deal/OPSLY-2028-001
Doc: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c

If this looks right I’ll calendar the 8/8 slot and send a short invite. Thanks — excited to get Opsly started.

—
Marissa Cole
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0183
Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Alicia Tran <alicia.tran@opsly.ai>, Devon Lee <devon@opsly.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 1 Aug 2028 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Opsly pilot: deployment preference & quick sandbox endpoints

Hi Alicia — quick technical reply to the two questions you called out.

1) Sandbox isolation / keys
- Yes: Hosted sandbox workspaces are client-isolated. We provision a separate API key and sandbox project for Opsly so you can run redacted production-like traffic without cross-tenant exposure. Keys can be rotated and scoped to sandbox endpoints.

2) Conservative Hosted rate limits (recommended initial config)
- Baseline: 20 requests/sec aggregated for the sandbox project (shared across endpoints).
- Burst window: allow up to 60 req/sec sustained for short bursts (30s) with queued backpressure beyond that.
- Per-route token cap: 2,000 tokens per request to prevent runaway costs during pilot.

Additional details:
- Latency: with prefix/KV caching enabled we typically see median latency drop 30–40% for repetitive prompts; P95 at the traffic profile you shared (10 avg / 40 peak, ~600 tokens) should be in the ~200–320ms window on Hosted depending on model choice.
- If you need stricter tenancy or reserved throughput (no noisy neighbors), we can outline a Dedicated commitment path — that typically includes reserved GPU pools and a migration playbook (we can share that if you want to see costs/lead times).

Attached is a slimmed endpoint map (same workbook Marissa attached) and a short checklist for the ops call. Can we lock Tue 8/8 10am PT for the 30-min call? I’ll prepare sample cURLs and a small test script to simulate your peak bursts.

—

source.subject: Next steps for ZenCommerce hosted evaluation: endpoints, rate limits & quickstart
source.thread:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Alex Li <alex@zencommerce.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 09 Jul 2028 15:12:00 +0000
Subject: Next steps for ZenCommerce hosted evaluation: endpoints, rate limits & quickstart

Hi Alex —

Thanks again for taking the demo earlier today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum: 

1) Goal: get a small hosted evaluation running (sandbox) so your integrations team can exercise the endpoints, measure latency at your typical payloads, and validate prefix/KV cache behavior.
2) What we need from you: a short traffic profile (avg tokens per call, percentile sequence lengths, expected RPS during the trial) and any compliance/residency constraints.
3) Redwood deliverables: sandbox endpoints, a short quickstart checklist (attached), a transient API key and a rate-slice proposal (start: 5 RPS sustained, bursts to 20 qps for short bursts).

Open questions for ZenCommerce:
- Do you expect EU data residency requirements for this evaluation or is US-only fine for the sandbox?
- Can your team sustain 5 RPS steady for two weeks? If not, what slice is realistic for the initial run?

I've attached a short hosted quickstart checklist that walks through endpoints, a suggested test harness, and quick validation steps for caching/batching. If this sounds right I'd like to schedule a 30-minute kickoff with your integrations lead to provision the sandbox and handoff credentials.

Available times: Tue/Wed morning (PST) or Thu afternoon.

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123

Attachment: redwood-hosted-quickstart-checklist.pdf (1 page)

---

From: Alex Li <alex@zencommerce.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Narayanan <priya@zencommerce.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Jul 2028 09:20:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Next steps for ZenCommerce hosted evaluation: endpoints, rate limits & quickstart

Thanks Ben — good demo. A few clarifications from our side: 

1) Residency: for the evaluation we are fine with US-only sandbox, but our legal team will need to see the retention and deletion policy before we move to production (likely EU residency requirement later).
2) Traffic profile: our initial tests will be a mix of 2–8 RPS depending on batch jobs; typical query length is 60–120 tokens with occasional 800-token threads for conversation history. We can commit to a 5 RPS steady slice for the two-week pilot if we can have predictable latency (p50 < 200ms, p95 < 600ms).
3) Endpoints: Which endpoints will we get? Do you provide separate chat vs completion endpoints and a dedicated embeddings endpoint? Any per-endpoint rate caps?

Can we do kickoff Wednesday 10:00 AM PST? Priya (cc'd) will bring our integrations lead. Also please send the trial API key TTL and an attachment with example cURL requests.

— Alex
Alex Li
Head of Integrations | ZenCommerce
alex@zencommerce.com

---

On Tue, Jul 10, 2028 at 16:45 +0000, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:

Hi Alex / Priya —

Great, thanks for the details — that usage profile is helpful. Answers below and next steps: 

Endpoints and rate caps:
- Chat API (streaming) and Completion API (non-streaming) will be provisioned as separate endpoints.
- Embeddings endpoint will be provisioned as well.
- For the sandbox we propose the following per-endpoint baselines: Chat: 5 RPS sustained per customer-slice, Completion: 5 RPS, Embeddings: 2 RPS. Bursts allowed up to 20 RPS for short windows (60s) subject to queueing. We can tweak these after early telemetry.

Latency SLOs:
- We aim to meet your targets on the hosted tier for the payloads you described. I will loop Marissa (solutions) and Sean (product) into the kickoff for tuning advice around prefix caching and batching.

Security/retention:
- Sandbox: short-lived keys, logs retained 7 days by default. Production options: retention and residency controls are part of our Private/Dedicated offerings. I'll attach the hosted quickstart checklist and a one-page note on retention (see attachment).

Kickoff & artifacts:
- Confirmed: Wed, 11 Jul 2028 10:00 AM PST (we will send a calendar invite shortly).
- Deliverables at kickoff: sandbox endpoints, API key (TTL 14 days), sample cURL and Postman collection, quickstart checklist (attached).
- Please share a HubSpot record if you want us to attach the trial artifacts; otherwise we'll upload logs to the shared Drive link.

Next steps for us (internal): Marissa will provision endpoints and generate sandbox credentials; Sean will prepare a short tuning note for your 800-token conversation cases.

Ben

Attachment: redwood-hosted-quickstart-checklist.pdf (1 page)
Attachment: sandbox-credentials-stub.txt (credentials redacted in thread)

---

From: Priya Narayanan <priya@zencommerce.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Alex Li <alex@zencommerce.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 11 Jul 2028 19:05:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Next steps for ZenCommerce hosted evaluation: endpoints, rate limits & quickstart

Thanks — Wed 10:00 AM PST works for us. Two quick asks before the kickoff so our engineering manager can prioritize: 

- Can Redwood confirm SLA-ish expectations for the hosted evaluation (we understand sandbox is not SLA-backed, but a short note on expected uptime and fallback behavior would help our risk folks)?
- We would like the sandbox to allow a single sustained 5 RPS slice with a max 60s burst to 20 RPS and discrete per-endpoint caps as Ben outlined. Please confirm you can toggle those caps if we need a short expansion window.

If you can answer those briefly in the calendar invite or in the kickoff, we should be good to start. Looking forward to the call.

Best,
Priya
Engineering Manager | ZenCommerce
priya@zencommerce.com

[On Wed 11 Jul 2028 10:00:00 PST call scheduled — Redwood to send calendar invite with Dial-in + Drive link]

messages:
From: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Mon, 25 Jun 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Sprint exceptions reconciliation & QBR asks — next steps

Team —

Thanks for a tight sync this morning. Two quick asks to get us QBR-ready by next week: reconcile the open sprint exceptions and surface the top 3 exec asks we need visible on the QBR deck. I uploaded a working tracker (qbr_sprint_exceptions_tracker.xlsx) to the shared Drive and attached a copy. Please do the following by EOD Wed: 

1) Mark rows as RESOLVED/IN-PROGRESS and add a short owner + ETA.
2) For any exception that risks QBR messaging (latency regressions, model-quality regressions, infra exceptions), add a single-line impact statement we can copy into the deck.
3) Each of you send me one high-priority exec ask (30–60 words) that we can include on the QBR slide.

Key context: Acme Analytics (related_account) expects a dedicated rollout window in Q3; procurement has asked for a security addendum (attached). We need final alignment on rollout SLA and communication plan.

I'll consolidate everything into a one-page rollup on Thu morning. If there are gating items for procurement/security, loop Jessica (Acme) and Samir (integration partner).

--
Sean Gallagher
Head of Customer Reliability
Redwood Inference

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 25 Jun 2028 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Sprint exceptions reconciliation & QBR asks — next steps

Sean —

Thanks, uploaded my pass on the tracker. Highlights: 
- Row 12 (model-card drift) -> OWNER: Marissa, ETA: 2028-06-30, IMPACT: "Potential 8–12% drop in relevance for ad-hoc prompts; visible in customer A/B test"
- Row 7 (kv-cache perf spike) -> OWNER: Karthik, ETA: 2028-07-03, IMPACT: "Transient 50 ms tail latency increase for long sequences"

One exec ask from my side (for the QBR slide): "Approve 4-week pilot for dedicated caching tier for top-tier customers to stabilize tail latency and reduce token costs."

I'm attaching a brief note in the tracker and flagged two rows as procurement blockers (rows 2 & 9). Also linking SUP ticket: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/SUP-2345 (details in tracker comments).

On the security addendum: I reviewed acme_security_addendum.pdf and called out three items for legal/security to confirm — I'll drop a short summary by Tue morning.

-Marissa
Product Ops

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 26 Jun 2028 08:27:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Sprint exceptions reconciliation & QBR asks — next steps

Quick clarification on Row 7 (kv-cache perf spike): the spike correlates to a new batching heuristic we rolled on Jun 20. I'm opening a short investigation task and assigning Mina to validate whether the change altered 95th percentile behavior across seq-length cohorts.

Mina — can you confirm by Wed EOD if this is a heuristic regression or a load pattern change? If heuristic, we should consider a targeted rollback for the Acme rollout window.

My exec ask for the QBR: "Authorize a temporary rollback path and canary toggle for the new batching heuristic to protect committed SLAs during customer pilots."

- Karthik
Senior Engineering Manager

On Mon, 25 Jun 2028 at 11:03, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com> wrote:
> Sean —
> 
> Thanks, uploaded my pass on the tracker....

From: Jessica Park <jessica@acme-analytics.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement <procurement@acme-analytics.com>, Samir Rao <samir@dataform.co>

source.subject: OrchardLane integration qualification: vectors, batching, migration next steps
messages:
From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>
To: Taylor Brooks <taylor@orchardlane.com>, Priya Shah <priya@orchardlane.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-14T14:02:00Z
Subject: OrchardLane discovery recap + integration qualification items

Thanks for taking the time today — quick recap and proposed next steps from our side. High-level takeaways:

- Primary use case: catalog search + personalized ranking for recommendations. Reranker sits downstream of embeddings and returns a 0-1 score.
- Current provider: VectorCloud (we learned they use 1536-dim floats, hosted index).
- Target latency SLO: 60ms p99 for single-query rerank path; bulk batch jobs can be longer.
- Security: customer-managed encryption keys (KMS) and audit logs required.

Technical qualification items we need to validate for a dedicated / private deployment:
1) Vector dimensionality: are you willing to re-index to a smaller dim (768 or 1024) if we demonstrate cost/latency wins? Or do you need parity with 1536 initially? Please confirm tolerances.
2) Normalization: do you currently L2-normalize embeddings before indexing? If not, we typically recommend unit normalization when using cosine rerankers — we can evaluate baseline impact with sample vectors.
3) Batching cadence: expected QPS vs batch window for bulk ingest. For real-time traffic, suggested continuous micro-batching (1–4ms batching window) to meet latency targets. For migration backfills we recommend 5–20k vectors per job depending on infra.
4) Auth & SDK: which SDKs will your infra teams use? We support Python/Node/Go and have ready snippets for token-based API keys plus mutual TLS for Private.
5) Tooling & structured output: reranker will return structured score + metadata. Any preferred schema? We'll propose a default JSON envelope for tool-calling.

Next steps (proposed):
- Taylor/Priya: share a sample of 1k vectors + embedding metadata (dim, dtype, per-dim stats) — you mentioned a CSV export; that will help us run a quick eval.
- Redwood: send an integration checklist, SDK snippet for auth and a short migration playbook (small sample + full index cutover).
- Schedule a 60-minute deep-dive next week for architecture and migration sequencing.

I attached a quick security checklist and flagged the items we can cover in Dedicated/Private.

Thanks — Hannah
Hannah Schmitt | Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
hannah.schmitt@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123
Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: orchardlane-security-summary.pdf (summary of required compliance items)
From: Taylor Brooks <taylor@orchardlane.com>
To: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Shah <priya@orchardlane.com>
Date: 2028-06-14T17:30:00Z
Subject: Re: OrchardLane discovery recap + integration qualification items

Hannah — thanks for the clear recap. Confirming items from our side:

- We can export a 1k sample today (attached as current-embed-stats.csv). The full index is ~18M vectors at 1536 dims.
- Our initial preference is to preserve 1536 dims for parity, but we are open to an experiment where Redwood recommends a smaller dim (768/1024) and we measure retrieval and reranking delta on a holdout set.
- We currently do NOT L2-normalize before indexing — our scoring pipeline applies dot-product with learned weights in the reranker. We are concerned that changing normalization might require minor retraining.
- Batching: production QPS is ~25 qps on the rerank endpoint (95% single-qps spikes to 80 qps). Bulk backfills run overnight with jobs of ~50k vectors.
- Auth: we'll start with API keys and evaluate mTLS for Private.

Questions:
1) If we keep 1536 dims, what are the expected p50/p95 token costs and latency delta vs 1024?
2) Does Redwood offer an automated normalization shim that can emit normalized vectors into the index while preserving original vectors for A/B?

We'll schedule the deep-dive — available Tue/Wed next week afternoon PT.

Thanks — Taylor
Taylor Brooks | Senior Eng Manager, Data Platforms
Taylor.Brooks@orchardlane.com | 510-555-0199

--
Attachment: current-embed-stats.csv
From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
To: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Taylor Brooks <taylor@orchardlane.com>
Date: 2028-06-14T21:05:00Z
Subject: Re: OrchardLane discovery recap + integration qualification items

Looping in a couple notes from my side — quick engineering-first answers to their questions. Replying inline to help the architecture convo: 

> "If we keep 1536 dims, what are the expected p50/p95 token costs and latency delta vs 1024?"
- CPU/GPU memory and compute scale roughly linearly with vector length for the NN search phase; switching 1536 -> 1024 typically reduces inference latency ~20-30% and memory footprint ~33% for pure dense ops depending on kernel. Exact p95 depends on index type (HNSW vs IVF+PQ) and quantization. We can run a bench on a 1k sample and return numbers.

> "Does Redwood offer an automated normalization shim..."
- We have an ingestion pipeline stage option: "normalization layer" that can emit normalized vectors to a side index for A/B. That avoids immediate retraining of downstream rerankers while you run comparative evals. Implementation notes: side-index can be flagged in routing rules so 10% of traffic goes to normalized pipeline.

Recommendations for migration approach:
1) Smoke test: run embedding inference for 1k sample through Redwood SDK (we'll provide python snippet) and store side-index of normalized 1k to compare similarity metrics.
2) Backfill strategy: parallelizable jobs of 50k with checkpointed progress; recommend starting with a staged cutover by namespace (region/customer) to limit blast radius.
3) Batch sizing: for real-time path we recommend micro-batches of 8–32 for tokenization/inference and continuous batching window of 2–5ms for the sampler. For backfills keep job size at 20–50k indexed vectors per worker depending on VM/GPU profile.
4) Reranker compatibility: if you normalize vectors, retraining may be needed but we expect only small delta if you use cosine-based models; dot-product models require careful conversion — we should run an A/B with holdout.

Action items for us: I'll prepare a simple benchmark using their 1k sample, include p50/p95 latency and cost delta for 1536 vs 1024 and a short playbook for side-indexing + routing. Karthik — can you draft the python SDK snippet for normalization + side-index flag?

Sean
Sean Gallagher | Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
sean.gallagher@redwood.com

> On Jun 14, 2028, at 14:02, Hannah Schmitt wrote:
> > (original message trimmed)
From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>
To: Taylor Brooks <taylor@orchardlane.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-15T09:17:00Z
Subject: Re: OrchardLane discovery recap + integration qualification items

Perfect — thanks Taylor and Sean. I scheduled a 60-min architecture deep-dive on Wed 2028-06-21 at 14:00 PT (invite sent). Agenda: 1) bench results on 1k sample (Sean) 2) demo SDK snippet + normalization side-index (Karthik) 3) migration sequencing and cutover checklist (me).

I uploaded an integration checklist and the sample upload instructions here: https://drive.redwood.com/drive/folders/1aA_orchardlane-integration and linked the HubSpot record: https://hubspot.redwood/in/ORCH-3421.

Taylor — could you confirm you shared the 1k CSV and the overnight backfill size expectation (you mentioned 50k jobs)? Once we have the sample I’ll run the bench and share initial numbers before the call.

Thanks — Hannah

--
Calendar: https://calendar.redwood.com/event/arch-deepdive-orchardlane

- We currently do NOT L2-normalize before indexing — our scoring pipeline applies dot-product with learned weights in the reranker. We are concerned that changing normalization might require minor retraining.
- Batching: production QPS is ~25 qps on the rerank endpoint (95% single-qps spikes to 80 qps). Bulk backfills run overnight with jobs of ~50k vectors.
- Auth: we'll start with API keys and evaluate mTLS for Private.

Questions:
1) If we keep 1536 dims, what are the expected p50/p95 token costs and latency delta vs 1024?
2) Does Redwood offer an automated normalization shim that can emit normalized vectors into the index while preserving original vectors for A/B?

We'll schedule the deep-dive — available Tue/Wed next week afternoon PT.

Thanks — Taylor
Taylor Brooks | Senior Eng Manager, Data Platforms
Taylor.Brooks@orchardlane.com | 510-555-0199

--
Attachment: current-embed-stats.csv


From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
To: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Taylor Brooks <taylor@orchardlane.com>
Date: 2028-06-14T21:05:00Z
Subject: Re: OrchardLane discovery recap + integration qualification items

Looping in a couple notes from my side — quick engineering-first answers to their questions. Replying inline to help the architecture convo: 

> "If we keep 1536 dims, what are the expected p50/p95 token costs and latency delta vs 1024?"
- CPU/GPU memory and compute scale roughly linearly with vector length for the NN search phase; switching 1536 -> 1024 typically reduces inference latency ~20-30% and memory footprint ~33% for pure dense ops depending on kernel. Exact p95 depends on index type (HNSW vs IVF+PQ) and quantization. We can run a bench on a 1k sample and return numbers.

> "Does Redwood offer an automated normalization shim..."
- We have an ingestion pipeline stage option: "normalization layer" that can emit normalized vectors to a side index for A/B. That avoids immediate retraining of downstream rerankers while you run comparative evals. Implementation notes: side-index can be flagged in routing rules so 10% of traffic goes to normalized pipeline.

Recommendations for migration approach:
1) Smoke test: run embedding inference for 1k sample through Redwood SDK (we'll provide python snippet) and store side-index of normalized 1k to compare similarity metrics.
2) Backfill strategy: parallelizable jobs of 50k with checkpointed progress; recommend starting with a staged cutover by namespace (region/customer) to limit blast radius.
3) Batch sizing: for real-time path we recommend micro-batches of 8–32 for tokenization/inference and continuous batching window of 2–5ms for the sampler. For backfills keep job size at 20–50k indexed vectors per worker depending on VM/GPU profile.
4) Reranker compatibility: if you normalize vectors, retraining may be needed but we expect only small delta if you use cosine-based models; dot-product models require careful conversion — we should run an A/B with holdout.

Action items for us: I'll prepare a simple benchmark using their 1k sample, include p50/p95 latency and cost delta for 1536 vs 1024 and a short playbook for side-indexing + routing. Karthik — can you draft the python SDK snippet for normalization + side-index flag?

Sean
Sean Gallagher | Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
sean.gallagher@redwood.com

> > (original message trimmed)

text:
From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>
To: Taylor Brooks <taylor@orchardlane.com>, Priya Shah <priya@orchardlane.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-14T14:02:00Z
Subject: OrchardLane discovery recap + integration qualification items

Thanks for taking the time today — quick recap and proposed next steps from our side. High-level takeaways:

- Primary use case: catalog search + personalized ranking for recommendations. Reranker sits downstream of embeddings and returns a 0-1 score.
- Current provider: VectorCloud (we learned they use 1536-dim floats, hosted index).
- Target latency SLO: 60ms p99 for single-query rerank path; bulk batch jobs can be longer.
- Security: customer-managed encryption keys (KMS) and audit logs required.

Technical qualification items we need to validate for a dedicated / private deployment:
1) Vector dimensionality: are you willing to re-index to a smaller dim (768 or 1024) if we demonstrate cost/latency wins? Or do you need parity with 1536 initially? Please confirm tolerances.
2) Normalization: do you currently L2-normalize embeddings before indexing? If not, we typically recommend unit normalization when using cosine rerankers — we can evaluate baseline impact with sample vectors.
3) Batching cadence: expected QPS vs batch window for bulk ingest. For real-time traffic, suggested continuous micro-batching (1–4ms batching window) to meet latency targets. For migration backfills we recommend 5–20k vectors per job depending on infra.
4) Auth & SDK: which SDKs will your infra teams use? We support Python/Node/Go and have ready snippets for token-based API keys plus mutual TLS for Private.
5) Tooling & structured output: reranker will return structured score + metadata. Any preferred schema? We'll propose a default JSON envelope for tool-calling.

Next steps (proposed):
- Taylor/Priya: share a sample of 1k vectors + embedding metadata (dim, dtype, per-dim stats) — you mentioned a CSV export; that will help us run a quick eval.
- Redwood: send an integration checklist, SDK snippet for auth and a short migration playbook (small sample + full index cutover).
- Schedule a 60-minute deep-dive next week for architecture and migration sequencing.

I attached a quick security checklist and flagged the items we can cover in Dedicated/Private.

Thanks — Hannah
Hannah Schmitt | Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
hannah.schmitt@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123
Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: orchardlane-security-summary.pdf (summary of required compliance items)

source.subject: Service Notice: Intermittent request duplication and elevated p95 in US-East
messages:
From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
To: Alex Torres <alex@cedarlabs.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Jun 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Service Notice: Intermittent request duplication and elevated p95 in US-East

Alex —

Quick heads up: between ~08:40–09:05 PDT we observed intermittent duplicate responses (identical token sequences returned twice) for a subset of requests routed to our US-East pool. Concurrently p95 latency for generation calls for your account spiked from ~420ms to ~1.8s for a small percentage (<1.2%) of requests.

What we know so far:
- Scope: Primarily affects requests hitting us-east-1 model pool (multi-tenant hosted API).
- Symptom: occasional duplicate payloads returned followed by a successful response; duplicates were identical and safe but caused retries/incremental processing on your side.
- Impact window: ~08:38–09:07 PDT.
- Customers affected: we see higher signal for CedarLabs (your traffic patterns and batching shapes), but other customers with similar request shapes were minimally affected.

Immediate mitigations we initiated:
- Manually routed new CedarLabs traffic away from the most impacted US-East sub-pool to US-West for a safety window.
- Engaged the runtime on-call and throttled a small set of automated internal retries that were amplifying the duplicate symptom.
- Collected perf samples and KV cache traces (attached).

Next steps:
- Our infra team is performing a kernel/connection-level investigation; we expect an initial update in ~60–90 minutes and will post a status page entry.
- If you see ongoing duplicates or any downstream errors, please reply with request IDs (we included an example in the attachment).

Apologies for the disruption — we realize how even infrequent duplicates can break ingestion pipelines. We are prioritizing a root-cause and will follow up with a postmortem and actions.

Attachments: perf-sample-cedarlabs-20280610.txt, us-east-reroute-runbook-20280610.pdf

— Kimberly Park
Director, Customer Reliability
Redwood Inference
From: Alex Torres <alex@cedarlabs.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Cc: CedarLabs Engineering <eng@cedarlabs.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Jun 2028 09:25:37 -0700
Subject: Re: Service Notice: Intermittent request duplication and elevated p95 in US-East

Kimberly —

Thanks for the quick note. We did see a spike in duplicate events in our consumer telemetry around 08:45–09:00 PDT and it caused a brief backlog in our ingestion workers (we dedupe by id but the duplicates trip a secondary path). Not catastrophic, but enough to surface errors and extra charges on our side for retries.

We noticed a couple of request IDs in the log: CL-20280610-7a2f, CL-20280610-7a31 — attaching a small sample. Our team appreciates the manual reroute to US-West, please confirm if you anticipate any client-side changes.

Also please flag if there will be any credit or bill adjustments — we'll need to reconcile retry-driven token usage for the window.

— Alex Torres
Head of Platform Engineering, CedarLabs
From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Jun 2028 12:03:10 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Service Notice: Intermittent request duplication and elevated p95 in US-East

Folks — forwarding CedarLabs reply and sample IDs. Summary for ops:

- Impact: duplicates caused modest ingestion backlog at customer; likely amplified by aggressive client retries.
- Hypothesis: connection-level ACK/race during rapid KV cache refresh resulted in duplicate response emission under certain batching boundaries.
- Immediate action taken: manual route, throttled internal retry loop, captured traces and perf-samples (see attachments).

Plan (short):
1) Engineering to triage kernel/transport logs and confirm whether a race in the response flush path caused duplicate writes. (Sean)
2) Product ops to draft status page entry and customer-facing note with recommended client-side backoff guidance. (Marissa)
3) Finance/sales to evaluate eligibility for a small token/ingestion credit for CedarLabs given measurable retry-driven costs. (Kimberly to coordinate with sales)

Please add to the incident channel and propose a postmortem owner. I recommend Sean lead the triage and Marissa draft the status update.

On Mon, Jun 10, 2028 at 09:12, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com> wrote:
> Alex —
> 
> Quick heads up: between ~08:40–09:05 PDT we observed intermittent duplicate responses...

— Kimberly
From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
To: Alex Torres <alex@cedarlabs.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Jun 2028 16:03:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Service Notice: Intermittent request duplication and elevated p95 in US-East — follow-up

Alex —

Thanks again for the sample IDs and the quick validation. Final update while we complete the internal postmortem:

- Mitigation status: The manual reroute to US-West remained in place until 10:20 PDT and eliminated duplicates for CedarLabs traffic. We removed the throttle and restored normal routing after a safe validation window.
- Root-cause (interim): Early signals point to a race during cache invalidation where the response pipeline could emit a residual buffer while a retry path also replayed the request. We have a reproducible test-case and a proposed kernel-level fix queued for the next runtime release.
- Customer impact: brief ingestion backlog and ~0.9% higher token consumption for the affected window. We are preparing a small credit offer to offset retry costs.
- Next steps: publish a concise incident post on status.redwood.inference (link below) and a longer postmortem within 72 hours that includes timeline, root cause, and actions (patch, additional testing, improved client backoff guidance).

Status page draft (for your review):
"2028-06-10 08:38–09:07 PDT: Intermittent duplicate responses and p95 latency spike affecting a subset of requests in US-East. Mitigations: manual routing and throttle; full recovery validated by 10:20 PDT. Full postmortem forthcoming."

Status page link: https://status.redwood.inference/incidents/2028-06-us-east-p95-duplication

If you want the credit request initiated now, reply and we will loop finance and your AE. Otherwise we will include the credit proposal in the postmortem.

Appreciate your patience — we will share the full postmortem and any runtime patch notes once available.

— Kimberly Park
Director, Customer Reliability
Redwood Inference

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
To: Alex Torres <alex@cedarlabs.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Jun 2028 16:03:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Service Notice: Intermittent request duplication and elevated p95 in US-East — follow-up

Alex —

Thanks again for the sample IDs and the quick validation. Final update while we complete the internal postmortem:

- Mitigation status: The manual reroute to US-West remained in place until 10:20 PDT and eliminated duplicates for CedarLabs traffic. We removed the throttle and restored normal routing after a safe validation window.
- Root-cause (interim): Early signals point to a race during cache invalidation where the response pipeline could emit a residual buffer while a retry path also replayed the request. We have a reproducible test-case and a proposed kernel-level fix queued for the next runtime release.
- Customer impact: brief ingestion backlog and ~0.9% higher token consumption for the affected window. We are preparing a small credit offer to offset retry costs.
- Next steps: publish a concise incident post on status.redwood.inference (link below) and a longer postmortem within 72 hours that includes timeline, root cause, and actions (patch, additional testing, improved client backoff guidance).

Status page draft (for your review):
"2028-06-10 08:38–09:07 PDT: Intermittent duplicate responses and p95 latency spike affecting a subset of requests in US-East. Mitigations: manual routing and throttle; full recovery validated by 10:20 PDT. Full postmortem forthcoming."

Status page link: https://status.redwood.inference/incidents/2028-06-us-east-p95-duplication

If you want the credit request initiated now, reply and we will loop finance and your AE. Otherwise we will include the credit proposal in the postmortem.

Appreciate your patience — we will share the full postmortem and any runtime patch notes once available.

— Kimberly Park
Director, Customer Reliability
Redwood Inference

messages:
From: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-05T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: NovusCare pilot: schema-enforced structured output — request for sprint lift

Evelyn / team,

Quick note from the NovusCare pilot front — Samantha and their clinical integrations lead (cc'd on the thread) are asking for a schema-enforced structured-output feature so their EHR ingestion pipeline can map tokens to discrete fields (patient_id, encounter_date, ICD_codes). This is blocking the pilot acceptance criteria for their pilot starting mid-July.

Why this matters: without deterministic structured output we can't guarantee the downstream mapping, which is a regulatory risk for them. Winning this pilot unlocks a potential $600k ARR expansion next year.

Ask: can Product/Eng consider a sprint lift to slot a scoped implementation (basic JSON-schema enforcement + deterministic fail path) in the next 2-week sprint window? Proposed minimal acceptance criteria attached in the MAPP and the field mapping sheet.

Attachments: NovusCare_MutualActionPlan_v3.pdf; NovusCare_EHR_field_mapping.xlsx

If we need to sweeten with a short pilot fee/PO to prioritize, NovusCare has indicated willingness to sign a paid pilot. Samantha, can you confirm that?

Thanks — I can jump on a scoping call with Product today if useful.

—Sean
Sean Gallagher
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
sean@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0142
From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus@redwood.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-05T11:04:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: NovusCare pilot: schema-enforced structured output — request for sprint lift

Irene / Markus — forwarding Sean's note. High level: NovusCare (strategic pilot) needs a guaranteed structured output path for EHR ingestion (JSON schema enforcement + deterministic fail/fallback) to meet their compliance gate. They are willing to cover pilot engineering time (per Sean), and the MAPP + field mapping are attached.

Questions for Product/Eng: 1) rough effort estimate for a scoped implementation that covers schema enforcement and deterministic error handling; 2) dependencies (runtime changes, SDKs, telemetry); 3) risk to latency/throughput SLOs.

Can you reply with options (minimal viable shipping approach + medium/high effort) and whether a one-sprint carve-out is remotely possible? If not, what tradeoffs would get us into pilot in 3–4 weeks?

Thanks — Evelyn
Evelyn Hart
Senior Customer Success Manager | Redwood Inference
evelyn@redwood.com
From: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-05T14:28:00-07:00
Subject: Re: NovusCare pilot: schema-enforced structured output — request for sprint lift

Thanks for the context — this is helpful. A few clarifying items before I scope:

- Can we get a concrete example prompt + expected schema for 2–3 canonical workflows? The attached EHR mapping looks good but examples will help engineering tests.
- What is the acceptable failure behavior? (e.g., block the response and surface an error, return best-effort with a confidence flag, or fall back to unstructured output)
- Latency SLO: are they OK with a 10–15% p99 hit for schema validation, or do they require sub-ms additional overhead?
- Acceptance test: will NovusCare run a validation dataset and sign off on deterministic outputs?

Initial thought: two approaches — (A) build a lightweight schema enforcement in the runtime that validates tokens against a JSON schema and returns structured JSON (2–3 sprints), or (B) implement a validation proxy layer in front of the API that enforces/reformats responses (1 sprint for a scoped POC, but less integrated).

I can run a quick scoping workshop today at 3pm PT if that works for NovusCare + Eng. Sean / Evelyn: please share sample prompts and the customer's acceptance criteria.

—Irene
Irene Choi
Product Manager, Redwood Inference
irene@redwood.com
From: Markus Klein <markus@redwood.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-06T08:51:00-07:00
Subject: Re: NovusCare pilot: schema-enforced structured output — request for sprint lift

Quick engineering read — thanks for pulling this together. Some constraints and options from Eng:

- Full runtime-integrated schema enforcement that preserves streaming and low latency will likely take 3 sprints (design, implementation, tests). Impact to p99 depends on current model tokenization patterns; worst case ~20% p99 increase without further optimization.
- A validation/proxy layer that operates post-response (ingest -> validate/transform) can be delivered as a scoped service and automated test harness in 1 sprint. Tradeoffs: less elegant (no streaming structured output), but deterministic and lower risk to the serving runtime.
- If we consider a hybrid (runtime flag for structured output + proxy fallback), we can aim for a 2-sprint delivery but it requires a prioritized slot and a short pilot PO to cover engineering prioritization.

Requests:
1) Engineering needs explicit acceptance criteria and a signed pilot commitment (covers engineering hours and CLA/legal review) before we bump a sprint slot.
2) Please open a Jira (I've added ENG-4321 as the placeholder) with sample prompts and target schema examples.

If the customer is willing to accept a proxy-based POC first, we can get something in a week to 10 days and then iterate to a runtime-integrated approach.

—Markus
Markus Klein
Engineering Lead, Runtime
markus@redwood.com
From: Samantha Li <samantha.li@novuscare.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement <procurement@novuscare.com>
Date: 2028-06-07T09:15:00-07:00
Subject: Re: NovusCare pilot: schema-enforced structured output — request for sprint lift

Thanks, team. Confirming NovusCare can sign a short paid pilot to cover scoped engineering work (we've budgeted for a POC fee). We prefer the proxy POC first as long as it produces deterministic JSON we can validate against our mapping table.

We've attached an updated MAPP with milestones and a PO template — procurement is looped. Target start: week of 06/17 if engineering availability aligns. We need final dates to coordinate our integration test window (mid-July acceptance target).

Attachments: NovusCare_MutualActionPlan_v3.pdf

—Samantha Li
Director, Integrations | NovusCare Health
samantha.li@novuscare.com
From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-09T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: NovusCare pilot: schema-enforced structured output — request for sprint lift

All — good momentum here. Action items I will own:
- Align with Procurement to accept NovusCare PO and confirm pilot fee (EOD 6/10).
- Sean: please upload 2–3 sample prompts + expected JSON schema to the Jira ticket ENG-4321.
- Markus/Irene: can you confirm whether Eng can start on a proxy POC on 06/17 (one sprint) and provide a short spec by 06/12?

If Eng confirms the proxy POC and we have the PO, we can hit NovusCare's mid-July acceptance target. I'll coordinate an internal kickoff and the scoping call for 06/08 at 10am PT (invite to follow).

Marissa Cole
Customer Success Director | Redwood Inference
marissa@redwood.com

source.subject: Production degradation — credit and trial extension request
messages:
From: Jordan Park <ops@luminedge.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Shah <priya@luminedge.com>
Date: Tue, 07 May 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Production incident — May 6-7 token and latency spike

Hi Aditya,

We saw repeated 5-10s tail latencies and token throttles across our nightly inference jobs between 2028-05-06 22:30 UTC and 2028-05-07 03:15 UTC. This manifested as elevated error rates and several timeouts for customer-facing flows. We logged it as INC-2497 on our side.

Can you share what happened on Redwood's side, whether you have remediation logs, and whether there's scope for a credit/compensation for the impacted tokens? The outage caused a visible regression for a subset of our customers and we had to route traffic to a fallback.

Thanks — we need a ballpark by EOD to brief our leadership.

Jordan Park
Site Reliability Manager, LuminEdge
ops@luminedge.com

Attachments: (none)

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Park <ops@luminedge.com>, Priya Shah <priya@luminedge.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 07 May 2028 09:38:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Production incident — May 6-7 token and latency spike

Jordan — thanks for the heads up and really sorry for the disruption. We flagged increased queueing on a routing tier (service mesh + autoscale boundary) that coincided with a model reload; routing fallback hit slower CPU paths for ~4 hours.

Immediate next steps from our side:
- Postmortem in progress (P0) and I’ll share the findings tomorrow.
- We can apply an immediate token safety-net credit while we finish the recap (to minimize your billing shock).
- I’m proposing a preliminary offer: 125,000 free tokens + a 30-day trial extension on your current onboarding entitlement while we investigate. If that looks insufficient once we finish the analysis, we can adjust.

If that proposed remediation sounds ok, I’ll route for quick approval from pricing/ops and send a formal credit note.

— Aditya
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.com
On Tue, May 7, 2028 at 09:12 AM Jordan Park <ops@luminedge.com> wrote:
> We saw repeated 5-10s tail latencies and token throttles...

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 08 May 2028 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Production incident — May 6-7 token and latency spike

Thanks for the flag, Aditya. I reviewed the initial notes from infra — the incident aligns with a model reload edge-case that temporarily increased CPU-bound fallbacks. Given the customer impact and LuminEdge's expansion pipeline, I approve a more generous gesture: 150,000 tokens credit and a 45-day trial extension.

Marissa — please prepare a credit note and schedule the credit to appear in their billing (apply as account-level token credit, not invoice refund). Sean, please coordinate any SLA note if they ask for additional uptime credits.

Karthik
VP, Commercial Operations

source.subject: Re: Urgent: Hightide checkout p99 latency & 502s — suspected SDK stream parser bug
messages:
From: Elena Novak <elena@hightide.io>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Hightide SRE <sre@hightide.io>
Date: Thu, 02 May 2028 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Checkout spike / 502s — urgent

Jordan —

We started seeing p99 latency jump from ~450ms to >2.5s on our checkout endpoint at 14:50 PDT and a flood of 502s. This correlates with the 15:00 deploy on our side but we only changed a small client-side adapter. Our logs show a lot of half-complete streaming responses and then 502 from your gateway. SRE attached a sample log and a tcpdump capture of a failing session (pcap attached).

Can you confirm whether anything changed on {redwood} side? This is impacting a high-volume checkout flow — we may have to route away if this isn't stabilized within 4 hours.

— Elena
Elena Novak
Head of Platform, Hightide Commerce
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Elena Novak <elena@hightide.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 May 2028 15:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Checkout spike / 502s — urgent

Elena — thanks for the report and the captures. I’m escalating now to our on-call infra/serving team. No platform deploys on Redwood that coincide with 14:50 PDT for the Hightide tenant — we pushed a minor routing fix earlier in the day but it was rolled back at 09:00 PDT.

I’ve attached the files to our internal ticket and looped in Marissa (CS) and Sean (ops) so we can coordinate. Initial ask: can you share the exact SDK version and whether your adapter flips HTTP/1.1 <-> HTTP/2 mid-connection? Any recent changes to client-side connection pooling would help narrow repro.

I’ll update within 30 minutes.

— Jordan
Jordan Blake
Senior Account Exec, Redwood Inference
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 May 2028 15:48:00 -07:00
Subject: FW: URGENT: Hightide checkout p99 spike (customer-reported)

Karthik/Markus — forwarding Hightide's capture and sample logs (attached). Customer reports p99 >2.5s and many 502s starting 14:50 PDT. They indicate a small client adapter change that could be flipping between HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 for long-lived connections.

Quick observations from our gateway traces: we see partial streaming frames where the request headers arrive in multiple fragments and our parser logs show "incomplete header frame" followed by an immediate connection close on ~12% of sessions for this tenant. This pattern is concentrated on a subset of ingest nodes handling Hightide.

Request: can your team triage for a possible client-side streaming parser interaction (split headers across TCP frames) that leads to KV cache invalidation and subsequent tokenization/parsing failures? Customer is threatening to cut traffic. Prioritize please.

Attachments: hightide_5xx_sample.log, pcap_chunked_headers_capture.pcap, repro_playbook.md
From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 May 2028 16:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FW: URGENT: Hightide checkout p99 spike (customer-reported)

Thanks Jordan — I grabbed the pcap and logs. Early hypothesis: the client adapter is splitting multi-line headers and trailers across TCP frames in a way that interacts poorly with our client SDK incremental streaming parser. When the parser sees a header fragment containing only a continuation line it treats it as a malformed header and emits an internal parse-exit path that clears the request's KV cache entry. That cache clear then causes the serving runtime to fall back to cold-path tokenization on many subsequent requests, producing high CPU and tokenization latency and occasional 502s when the worker closes the stream mid-reassembly.

I can reproduce locally by forcing a chunked boundary that breaks a header name/value pair across two frames; the SDK parser currently expects a header line to arrive atomically (an assumption that held on older client behaviors). Repro steps and a small reproducer are in repro_playbook.md (attached).

Immediate mitigation: 1) we can push a routing rule to pin Hightide's traffic to nodes with a relaxed parser timeout; 2) recommend customer temporarily disable HTTP/2 multiplexing in their adapter (this avoids the split frame path). I’ll prepare a patch to the SDK parser to handle continuation fragments more gracefully; estimate 24–36 hours to cut a release and staged rollout.

Will post findings and a JIRA shortly (INF-2781).
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>, Victor Chan <victor.chan@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 03 May 2028 08:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: URGENT: Hightide checkout p99 spike (customer-reported)

Folks — customer reported that traffic decreased after they disabled HTTP/2 multiplexing per Karthik's suggestion. They are seeing reduced 502s but still slightly elevated p99. Hightide execs (Victor + Elena) requested written mitigation plan and asked about a service credit if SLA breaches are confirmed.

Sean — please prepare a concise RCA and proposed credit/compensation options. Jordan, prioritize a customer call for 09:30 PDT tomorrow to show the mitigation and timeline for the SDK patch. I’m putting this on Markus/Karthik's plate for technical writeup.

— Marissa
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Elena Novak <elena@hightide.io>, Victor Chan <victor.chan@hightide.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 04 May 2028 09:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Urgent: Hightide checkout p99 latency & 502s — suspected SDK stream parser bug

Elena/Victor — quick status: thank you for trying the temporary mitigation (disabling HTTP/2 multiplexing). Karthik's repro confirms an SDK parser edgecase where continuation header fragments can trigger cache-clear behavior in the serving path. We have a patch ready and QA has reproduced the fix across our scenarios. Plan: staged rollout starting 04 May 16:00 PDT to 10% internal traffic, 04 May 22:00 PDT to 50%, and full rollout 05 May 04:00 PDT, with an ability to rollback within 5 minutes.

We will also provide:
- A postmortem with timeline and root cause (by May 06).
- A proposed service credit for any verified SLA windows where p99 >1s (Sean will follow up with numbers).
- Guidance for client adapters to avoid mid-connection protocol flips and a small SDK compatibility note to make parser more tolerant.

Please confirm the 09:30 PDT call or propose another time. We appreciate the quick coordination.

— Jordan

Cc: Hightide SRE <sre@hightide.io>
Date: Thu, 02 May 2028 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Checkout spike / 502s — urgent

Jordan —

We started seeing p99 latency jump from ~450ms to >2.5s on our checkout endpoint at 14:50 PDT and a flood of 502s. This correlates with the 15:00 deploy on our side but we only changed a small client-side adapter. Our logs show a lot of half-complete streaming responses and then 502 from your gateway. SRE attached a sample log and a tcpdump capture of a failing session (pcap attached).

Can you confirm whether anything changed on {redwood} side? This is impacting a high-volume checkout flow — we may have to route away if this isn't stabilized within 4 hours.

— Elena
Elena Novak
Head of Platform, Hightide Commerce


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Elena Novak <elena@hightide.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 May 2028 15:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Checkout spike / 502s — urgent

Elena — thanks for the report and the captures. I’m escalating now to our on-call infra/serving team. No platform deploys on Redwood that coincide with 14:50 PDT for the Hightide tenant — we pushed a minor routing fix earlier in the day but it was rolled back at 09:00 PDT.

I’ve attached the files to our internal ticket and looped in Marissa (CS) and Sean (ops) so we can coordinate. Initial ask: can you share the exact SDK version and whether your adapter flips HTTP/1.1 <-> HTTP/2 mid-connection? Any recent changes to client-side connection pooling would help narrow repro.

I’ll update within 30 minutes.

— Jordan
Jordan Blake
Senior Account Exec, Redwood Inference


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 May 2028 15:48:00 -07:00
Subject: FW: URGENT: Hightide checkout p99 spike (customer-reported)

Karthik/Markus — forwarding Hightide's capture and sample logs (attached). Customer reports p99 >2.5s and many 502s starting 14:50 PDT. They indicate a small client adapter change that could be flipping between HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 for long-lived connections.

Quick observations from our gateway traces: we see partial streaming frames where the request headers arrive in multiple fragments and our parser logs show "incomplete header frame" followed by an immediate connection close on ~12% of sessions for this tenant. This pattern is concentrated on a subset of ingest nodes handling Hightide.

Request: can your team triage for a possible client-side streaming parser interaction (split headers across TCP frames) that leads to KV cache invalidation and subsequent tokenization/parsing failures? Customer is threatening to cut traffic. Prioritize please.

Attachments: hightide_5xx_sample.log, pcap_chunked_headers_capture.pcap, repro_playbook.md


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 May 2028 16:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FW: URGENT: Hightide checkout p99 spike (customer-reported)

Thanks Jordan — I grabbed the pcap and logs. Early hypothesis: the client adapter is splitting multi-line headers and trailers across TCP frames in a way that interacts poorly with our client SDK incremental streaming parser. When the parser sees a header fragment containing only a continuation line it treats it as a malformed header and emits an internal parse-exit path that clears the request's KV cache entry. That cache clear then causes the serving runtime to fall back to cold-path tokenization on many subsequent requests, producing high CPU and tokenization latency and occasional 502s when the worker closes the stream mid-reassembly.

I can reproduce locally by forcing a chunked boundary that breaks a header name/value pair across two frames; the SDK parser currently expects a header line to arrive atomically (an assumption that held on older client behaviors). Repro steps and a small reproducer are in repro_playbook.md (attached).

Immediate mitigation: 1) we can push a routing rule to pin Hightide's traffic to nodes with a relaxed parser timeout; 2) recommend customer temporarily disable HTTP/2 multiplexing in their adapter (this avoids the split frame path). I’ll prepare a patch to the SDK parser to handle continuation fragments more gracefully; estimate 24–36 hours to cut a release and staged rollout.

Will post findings and a JIRA shortly (INF-2781).


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>, Victor Chan <victor.chan@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 03 May 2028 08:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: URGENT: Hightide checkout p99 spike (customer-reported)

Folks — customer reported that traffic decreased after they disabled HTTP/2 multiplexing per Karthik's suggestion. They are seeing reduced 502s but still slightly elevated p99. Hightide execs (Victor + Elena) requested written mitigation plan and asked about a service credit if SLA breaches are confirmed.

Sean — please prepare a concise RCA and proposed credit/compensation options. Jordan, prioritize a customer call for 09:30 PDT tomorrow to show the mitigation and timeline for the SDK patch. I’m putting this on Markus/Karthik's plate for technical writeup.

— Marissa


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Elena Novak <elena@hightide.io>, Victor Chan <victor.chan@hightide.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 04 May 2028 09:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Urgent: Hightide checkout p99 latency & 502s — suspected SDK stream parser bug

Elena/Victor — quick status: thank you for trying the temporary mitigation (disabling HTTP/2 multiplexing). Karthik's repro confirms an SDK parser edgecase where continuation header fragments can trigger cache-clear behavior in the serving path. We have a patch ready and QA has reproduced the fix across our scenarios. Plan: staged rollout starting 04 May 16:00 PDT to 10% internal traffic, 04 May 22:00 PDT to 50%, and full rollout 05 May 04:00 PDT, with an ability to rollback within 5 minutes.

We will also provide:
- A postmortem with timeline and root cause (by May 06).
- A proposed service credit for any verified SLA windows where p99 >1s (Sean will follow up with numbers).
- Guidance for client adapters to avoid mid-connection protocol flips and a small SDK compatibility note to make parser more tolerant.

Please confirm the 09:30 PDT call or propose another time. We appreciate the quick coordination.

— Jordan

source.subject: [Urgent] p99 backpressure / errors on 32k long-window requests — Borealis Health
messages:
From: Marta Ruiz <marta.ruiz@borealishealth.com>
To: aisha_rahman@redwood.ai
Cc: infra-team@borealishealth.com
Date: 2028-04-11T07:10:00Z
Subject: [SEV] Sudden p99 spikes / 500s on long-window (32k) requests

Hi Aisha —

We started seeing elevated p99 latency and intermittent 500 responses from Redwood's hosted API this morning at ~07:02 UTC. The traffic patterns haven't changed on our side; these are the same 32k-context inference calls we've used in production for weeks. Impact: ~12% of inference calls are timing out or returning server errors (affecting live billing runs and downstream ingestion).

Attached: small excerpt of request IDs and a short trace (p99-trace snippet).

Quick details:
- Example request id: req_20280411_BH_0135
- Model pinned: rwd-llama-32k-v2 (hosted)
- Typical p95 before today: ~400ms; p99 usually <1.2s. Now p95 ~1.3s, p99 spiking to 8–12s; some 503/500s.
- No changes to prompt or input size on our side.

This is blocking our nightly pipeline. We need an ETA on mitigation and a short-term workaround. Please escalate — we may need exec attention if this will be unresolved for multiple hours.

Thanks,
Marta Ruiz
Head of Integrations, Borealis Health

Attachment: p99-trace-20280411.txt (excerpt)
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Marta Ruiz <marta.ruiz@borealishealth.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-04-11T07:25:00Z
Subject: Re: [SEV] Sudden p99 spikes / 500s on long-window (32k) requests

Hi Marta — thanks for the alert and for the trace. We're treating this as a priority.

Immediate steps we are taking now:
1) I've looped in our SRE/serving lead (Dev) and product lead (Sean).
2) Dev will run a focused trace across the request IDs you sent and check routing / queue depth for the 32k serving pool.
3) We'll propose a short-term mitigation (config change / fallback) within 30–45 minutes and follow with a rollback plan.

Can you confirm whether you see the failures only for the pinned model (rwd-llama-32k-v2) or also for shorter-window calls? That will help narrow whether this is model-specific or a request-size/queueing issue.

I'll keep you updated every 30 minutes until this is resolved.

— Aisha
Director, Customer Success — Enterprise
Redwood Inference
From: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.ai>
Cc: 
Date: 2028-04-11T09:08:00Z
Subject: RE: [SEV] Sudden p99 spikes / 500s on long-window (32k) requests — initial findings

Quick update from the runtime side (initial triage):

What I see:
- Around 07:00 UTC we observed a rapid increase in request concurrency for long-window requests on the hosted pool serving the rwd-llama-32k-v2 variant.
- Our continuous batching pipeline experienced backpressure; batch latency amplified non-linearly once in-memory KV cache hit-rate dropped (evictions due to larger-than-normal KV footprints).
- When KV cache eviction rate increased, we saw additional CPU/GPU context switches while reloading caches, which inflated p99. Some requests hit our hard timeout path and returned 500.

Immediate mitigations applied (07:40 UTC):
- Reduced max concurrent long-window requests per worker for the affected pool to throttle arrival.
- Enabled fallback routing to the 16k-optimized variant for requests that can tolerate truncation (config flag off-by-default).
- Purged a small stale cache segment that was contributing to evictions.

Attachments: metrics-screenshot-07am.png, p99-trace-20280411.txt (full).

Recommended short-term actions for Borealis:
- If acceptable, temporarily set request param allow_truncation=true for non-critical nightly batches to route to 16k variant. This avoids model swap but reduces context length.
- As a platform emergency measure we can roll a hotfix to change the fallback policy for your tenant only (non-global). That takes ~45m with a canary.

Longer term:
- We'll instrument finer-grained KV metrics and deploy an eviction-friendly cache policy for long-window traces; estimate 2–3 sprints for full coverage.

I'll keep watching and will post a follow-up with canary results.

— Dev
Senior SRE, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-04-11T10:12:00Z
Subject: Fwd: Exec note draft for Borealis — propose sending to Tom if we need credits

Folks —

We should prepare an exec-facing note and a commercial offer in case Borealis escalates to procurement for SLA credits. Draft below (attached separately). Please review quickly:

DRAFT to customer (editable):
——
Tom / Marta —

Thanks for flagging this. We understand the impact and apologize for the disruption. Our engineers have isolated the root cause to KV cache eviction pressure on the 32k serving pool and applied short-term mitigations (throttling and a tenant-only fallback to a 16k-optimized variant).

We will:
- Continue to monitor the canary for the next 2 hours and revert if we detect regressions.
- Provide a timeline for a permanent fix (KV eviction policy + instrumented metrics) within 72 hours.
- Offer a one-time credit equivalent to one week's hosted usage for the impacted tenant while we complete the fix (we can refine the amount with finance).

Best,
Aisha on behalf of Redwood Leadership
——

Please add suggested language re: credits and legal signoff if needed — we want to be ready if Tom asks for SLA credit and exec escalation.

— Aisha
From: Tom Hsu <tom.hsu@borealishealth.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>, Marta Ruiz <marta.ruiz@borealishealth.com>
Cc: infra-team@borealishealth.com
Date: 2028-04-11T12:03:00Z
Subject: Re: [SEV] Sudden p99 spikes / 500s on long-window (32k) requests

Appreciate the quick triage and the canary. Our infra team confirms that the bulk of errors were during the 07:00–08:10 UTC window and that the fallback to 16k kept the job progressing for non-critical batches.

Two asks:
1) Can you commit to a firm ETA for the permanent fix window (the 72-hour estimate is okay but we need a calendar date)?
2) We will expect a formal credit proposal from Redwood procurement that maps to impacted usage. Please put this in writing to our procurement contact (procure@borealishealth.com).

If you want to involve Redwood execs for faster approvals on credits, we can loop our VP of Platform.

— Tom
VP Product Ops, Borealis Health
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-04-11T15:42:00Z
Subject: Post-mortem next steps & commercial ask — Borealis Health

Summary and action items for internal follow-up (for the executive update):

Summary:
- Incident window: 2028-04-11T07:00 — 2028-04-11T08:40 UTC (most severe).
- Root cause: KV cache eviction pressure on the 32k-serving pool amplified batch latency under sudden concurrency spike.
- Short-term mitigations: tenant-only throttle + fallback to 16k variant (applied 07:40 UTC), purged stale cache segment. Canary looks stable; p99 returned to expected ranges by ~08:20 UTC.

Action items (owners & ETA):
- Dev: Ship eviction-friendly cache policy + additional telemetry to detect this pattern automatically (ETA: 2028-04-18).
- Sean: Draft the formal credit proposal and coordinate legal/finance signoff (ETA: 2028-04-12 EOD).
- Aisha: Send an exec note to Borealis (tom.hsu@borealishealth.com) with the proposed credit and timeline once Sean confirms numbers (ETA: 2028-04-11 17:00 UTC).
- Platform: Add tenant-scoped fallback capability to router config for similar long-window spikes (ETA: 2 weeks).

References: hubspot BH-8421, logs/drive link attached.

I'll prepare the exec note and run it by Evelyn for sign-off.

— Aisha
Director, Customer Success — Redwood Inference
